title: Spatial and particle-particle entanglement in 1D quantum walks of two distinguishable or indistinguishable bosonic particles
abstract: We present entanglement measures between spatially separated regions and between two distinguishable or indistinguishable particles in one-dimensional two-particle continuous-time quantum walks governed by the Hubbard Hamiltonian. The left-right entanglement checks the entropy of coarse-grained states counting the numbers of particles on the left and right halves of the lattice while the particle-particle entanglement is based on the entropy of the singular values of the time-evolved Fock state. With separable, entangled, and doubly occupied initial states, we examine initial entanglement and the following growth in different entanglement measures. While the entanglement measures of the indistinguishable cases resemble those of the distinguishable cases when the initial states are comparable, the long-time limits of the entanglement measures are typically non-monotonic as the onsite repulsion increases. We also discuss possible implications for future research of entanglement in multi-particle quantum dynamics.
summary: This paper analyzes the spatial and particle-particle entanglement in 1D quantum walks of two bosonic particles, comparing results for distinguishable and indistinguishable cases. Using various entanglement measures, the authors show that entanglement dynamics are highly sensitive to the onsite repulsion strength ($U/J$) and the initial state preparation. The findings highlight the general applicability of certain entanglement measures across particle statistics.
main_problem: The paper investigates the spatial and particle-particle entanglement dynamics in one-dimensional quantum walks involving two bosonic particles.
main_result: The entanglement measures exhibit non-monotonic behavior as the onsite repulsion ($U/J$) increases, and the left-right entanglement measure proves robustly applicable to both distinguishable and indistinguishable particle cases.
method: The study employs exact time evolution calculations using the time-evolution operator and calculates entanglement using measures derived from the entropy of coarse-grained states and singular value decompositions (SVD) of the Fock state coefficients.
model_or_system: The system models two bosonic particles undergoing continuous-time quantum walks on a 1D lattice, governed by a Hamiltonian related to the Bose-Hubbard model. The analysis compares results for distinguishable and indistinguishable particles.
key_observables: Left-right entanglement entropy ($E_{SLR}$), particle-particle entanglement (based on SVD of Fock state coefficients), and time-evolved occupation numbers $\langle n(x) angle$.
important_parameters: Onsite repulsion ratio ($U/J$), initial state type (separable, entangled, doubly occupied), and lattice size ($L=70$).
main_assumptions: The analysis focuses on two-particle systems to manage computational complexity, and the left-right entanglement measure is shown to be a generalizable tool for both particle types.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the time evolution of entanglement measures (Left-right and $\uparrow, \downarrow$) for various initial states and varying $U/J$ ratios for both distinguishable and indistinguishable particles, alongside plots of occupation number dynamics.
paper_structure: The paper systematically introduces the physical model and Hamiltonian, defines multiple entanglement measures for both particle types, and then analyzes the time evolution and dependence of these measures on initial conditions and the onsite repulsion strength ($U/J$).
why_interesting: This work provides a detailed quantitative comparison of entanglement dynamics in bosonic quantum walks, offering insights into how particle indistinguishability affects entanglement measures, which is crucial for understanding quantum simulation fidelity in many-body systems.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. (a) Left-right and (b) ↑, ↓entanglement measures for two distinguishable particles on a L = 70 lattice with the separable initial state of Eq. (8). The inset of (a) shows the probabilities of the |nL, nR⟩coarse-grained states taken at t/t0 = 20 for 0 < U/J ≤10. In both figures, the red dashed vertical line indicates the time when the particles reach the boundary and reflect off.

FIG. 2. (a) Left-right and (b) ↑, ↓entanglement measures for two distinguishable particles on a L = 70 lattice. The initial state is taken as the entangled state shown in Eq. (9). The inset in panel (a) shows the probabilities for the corse-grained states for 0 ≤U/t ≤10 taken at t/t0 = 20. For both panels, the red dashed vertical line indicates the time step at which the particles contact the boundary and reflect off.

FIG. 3. (a) Left-right and (b) ↑, ↓entanglement measures for two distinguishable particles on a L = 70 lattice. The initial state is the doubly occupied state shown in Eq. (10). The inset in panel (a) shows the probabilities for the corse- grained states for onsite repulsion values 0 ≤U/J ≤10 taken at t/t0 = 20. The red dashed line indicates the time when the particles first contact the boundary and reflect off. The orange dash-dotted line in panel (b) shows the case of a high onsite repulsion (U/J = 400).

FIG. 4. (a) Left-right and (b) particle-particle correlation measures for two indistinguishable particles in a lattice of size L = 70. The initial state is taken as the adjacent, singly occupied state given in Eq. (11). For both panels, the vertical red dashed line indicates the time step where the particles first reach the lattice boundary. The inset in panel (a) shows the probabilities for the corse-grained states for onsite repulsion values 0 ≤U/J ≤10 taken at t/t0 = 20.

FIG. 5. (a) Left-right and (b) particle-particle correlation measures for two indistinguishable particles in a lattice of size L = 70. The initial state is taken as the doubly occupied state of Eq. (12). The red dashed line indicates the time at which the particles first reach the boundary and reflect off. The inset in panel (a) shows the probabilities for the corse- grained states for onsite repulsion values 0 ≤U/t ≤10 taken at t/t0 = 20. The orange dash-dotted line in panel (b) shows the case of a high onsite repulsion (U/J = 300).
